title: Chapter Ten: The Rooftop Confession
content: The gala is bleeding out.

I can feel it from three floors up — the deceleration of bodies no longer dancing, the waitstaff clearing glasses with the efficient silence of people who've done this a thousand times, the string quartet packing their instruments. The party is a patient expiring quietly in a hospital bed, and I've left the room before anyone can ask me to stay.
The rooftop terrace is empty. Of course it is. It's November, and the cold has teeth.
I lean against the parapet and let it bite.

The wind comes off the Hudson and finds every gap in my dress — the open back, the slit along my thigh, the scandalous absence of anything between my shoulder blades. The cold presses its mouth to my spine and I shiver, but I don't go back inside. I breathe instead. Deep. The city smell fills me: exhaust and rain and the metallic tang of steam rising from grates thirty stories below. It smells like every city I've ever stood above. It smells like London in 1623 and Lisbon in 1789 and this same damned island a dozen times over, each iteration more cluttered, more electric, more alive.
My skin is still humming.

The dance. His hand at the small of my back. The way he moved like he'd been choreographing us in his head for weeks before I ever said yes. The heat of him through silk and wool. The hum — that low, persistent vibration I've lived with for four centuries — had surged during the waltz, had climbed my throat, had nearly pressed words out of me that I can never say. I'd smiled through it. I'd let him spin me. I'd kept my teeth together and my secrets behind them.
Now I'm here, and the wind is peeling the warmth off my skin layer by layer, and the hum is settling back to its usual simmer, and I can almost think.
Almost.
The door opens behind me.

I don't turn. I don't need to. There's a quality to the footstep — measured, deliberate, the walk of a man who has never once entered a room without considering what it means to be there. Rowan. Of course. He would follow. He's been following me all night with his eyes, and apparently now he's doing it with the rest of himself.
He stands beside me without speaking.

The silence that opens between us is warm. No — not warm. That's not right. It's the silence of two people who don't need to fill air with noise to know they're occupying the same space. It's the silence of a held breath. It's the most comfortable silence I've had in four hundred years, and that terrifies me more than anything else that's happened tonight.

Four hundred years of silences. Silences in bedchambers and carriages and borrowed apartments across a dozen lifetimes. Silences with lovers who looked at me and saw someone who no longer existed. Silences that ended in screams, in prayers, in the soft click of a door closing for the last time. And this — this quiet on a rooftop with a man I've known for three months — sits inside my chest like it's always been there. Like it was waiting for me.
I grip the parapet harder. The stone is rough under my palms. Grounding. Necessary.
"What are you afraid of?"

Rowan asks it to the city, not to me. His voice is even, but I can hear the architecture beneath it — the way he's been building this sentence all night, revising it, stripping the decoration, arriving at something bare and geometric and impossible to deflect. It's the voice of a man who designs things for a living. He's designed this question. It's load-bearing.
I consider the lie.

I've told so many. Four centuries of them, smooth as river stones, each one placed just where it needs to go. I'm from old money. I travel extensively. I was educated abroad. My family is gone. That last one is true, actually — time takes everyone eventually, takes them the way it can't take me. But the rest are architecture too, load-bearing walls holding up the house of someone who doesn't technically exist.
I consider the truth.

I've lived for four hundred years. My body changes. The person you're looking at right now is not the person I was, and she's not the person I'll become. I have loved across centuries and been called monster for it. I've watched the recognition drain from a lover's face like color from a photograph, and I have felt that drain in every cell of whatever body I was wearing at the time. I am a series of endings masquerading as a person.
I say something in between.
"Being loved by someone who will mourn the version of me they lost."

The wind takes the words before they can fall, and I watch them scatter out over the city like something dying. A pigeon banks between buildings. A taxi's headlights trace a curve through the grid. Below us, the traffic hums — a constant, low-frequency drone, the sound of ten thousand people trying to get somewhere else.

Rowan's brow furrows. I can see it in my peripheral vision — the way the line between his eyebrows deepens, the way his jaw shifts almost imperceptibly. He doesn't understand. But he feels the weight of it. Rowan has always been a man who weighs things — investments, decisions, the structural integrity of buildings and people alike. He knows this sentence is heavy even if he can't see what it's made of.
"What does that mean?"
I shake my head. The wind catches my hair, pulls it across my face, and I let it. I want the veil. "It means I'm not someone you can keep."

There. A smaller truth. A preview of the larger one. Enough to make him step back, to let me be the enigma he'll puzzle over for a few weeks and then file away under beautiful mistakes. Enough to end this before it becomes the kind of thing I can't survive.
Rowan doesn't step back.
He turns.

He turns and faces me fully, and the city lights halve his face — gold on the left, shadow on the right, like a coin standing on its edge. The effect is theatrical and entirely accidental, and it takes everything I have not to step backward, not to break this tableaux, not to flee into the warm lie of the party below.

"I've spent my entire life," he says, and his voice is different now. The measured architectural precision is fracturing. There's something underneath it — something seismic, something that's been pressing against the walls of his composure all night, maybe all month, maybe longer. "Building things I can keep. Companies. Collections. Reputations." He pauses. Each word costs him. I can see the price in the way his throat moves, the way his fingers curl and uncurl at his sides. "And none of it — none of it — has ever felt as real as standing next to you at a bar watching my own suit walk into a room."

His mouth twists — almost a smile, almost something else. The memory of it: the gallery opening, the whisky, the way I'd looked at his bespoke Rowan Ashworth original draped over my shoulders like it belonged there, and the way he'd stood next to me and watched his own creation choose me over him. The absurdity of it. The intimacy.

He's shaking. Slightly. The way a building shakes before you feel the tremor — a vibration you sense in your feet before your eyes register the sway. The control is cracking. I've seen Rowan command boardrooms, charm collectors, bend a room to his will with the quiet force of a man who never needs to raise his voice. I've never seen him shake.
"So whatever it is you think I can't handle," he says, and his eyes hold mine like he's anchoring himself, like if he looks away he'll lose the words entirely, "try me."
The words are right there.

They live in the space behind my sternum, in the hum that has lived there longer than most dynasties. I've lived for four hundred years. I've loved you in every century — I don't know how I know this, only that the hum surges when he enters a room the same way it surged when a blacksmith looked up from his anvil in York, when a painter tilted his head in Florence, when a soldier laughed in a trench outside Verdun. The same soul. The same gravitational pull. Every time, my body changes. Every time, he finds me. Every time, he loves the version, and every time, the version ends, and every time, the mourning in his eyes is the exact same color.

My body will change. You won't recognize me. You'll look at what I've become — the different face, the different hands, the different shape of the person who loves you — and you won't see me. You'll see an abomination. You'll see a thing that wore the person you loved like a stolen coat. That's what the last one called me. A stolen coat. A costume. A thing that took his wife away and left something else in her place.
I open my mouth.
The hum surges.

It seizes my throat like a hand closing, and I taste copper — bright and mineral and unmistakable, the taste of my own blood singing through me, the taste of four hundred years of almost-confessions, of swallowed truths, of words I've carried like stones in my mouth across continents and centuries. The hum will not let me speak. It has never let me speak. It is the covenant I never agreed to, the price I pay for a body that renews itself while the world rots around it.
I close my mouth.
"I can't."
Two syllables. The heaviest words I've ever said, and I've said words that ended wars. At least — small wars. Wars of the heart.

Rowan's jaw tightens. I watch the muscle there leap and settle, leap and settle, a piston of barely-contained frustration. His eyes search my face for the door, the handle, the way in. He's an architect. He builds entrances for a living. He can't find one in me.
"Can't or won't?"
"Both."

The word lands between us like something dropped from a great height. It doesn't shatter — it's too dense, too compressed, too full of everything I can't say. It just sits there, immutable, and we both look at it.

The wind shifts. It carries the smell of rain now, or the memory of rain — that metallic, asphalt scent that rises from city streets before the sky opens. I breathe it in and think about all the times I've stood on rooftops, in gardens, on balconies, breathing in the same smell. The world changes. The smell doesn't. Rain still smells like waiting.
Rowan reaches for my hand.

I see it coming. I have time to pull away — four hundred years of practice, four hundred years of stepping back before the touch can land, before the intimacy can root, before the loss can become inevitable. I have time to save myself.
I don't.

I let him take it. His fingers close around mine, warm and sure and slightly rough — the calluses of a man who still draws by hand, who still runs his thumb along the grain of wood and stone, who touches the world to confirm it's real. Our fingers intertwine. It shouldn't feel this simple. Nothing in my life has ever felt this simple.
We stand at the parapet, hands joined, watching the city.

The traffic hum drifts upward, indistinct and persistent, the sound of a world that doesn't stop moving regardless of what happens on its rooftops. The lights bloom and recede in the glass of the buildings across the park — offices still lit at midnight, apartments flickering with the blue pulse of televisions, the Occam's razor of a city that never sleeps slicing through the dark. Beautiful. Terrible. Alive in the way only a human construction can be alive — all that intention and energy and want stacked vertically into the sky.

Rowan's thumb moves across my knuckles. Small. Unconscious. The kind of gesture a person makes when they're not thinking about their hands, when their body is simply doing what their heart wants. It undoes me more than any grand declaration could.

"I've been looking for something my whole life," he says, and he says it to the city too, because sometimes you can only speak the truest things to a million strangers who can't answer back. "And I don't know what it is." He pauses. His thumb stills on my knuckle. Then, quietly, as if the volume of the truth embarrasses him: "But it feels like you."

There it is. The thing I've heard before — in a blacksmith's shop in York, in a painter's garret in Florence, in a bombed-out building in France. Different words. Different mouths. Same soul. Same certainty. The same way of finding me like a compass needle finds north, without understanding why, without being able to stop.
"That's the problem," I say.
I mean it more than he could possibly know.

He doesn't ask what I mean. Maybe he's learned that I won't answer. Maybe he understands that some truths need to be approached obliquely, the way you approach a wild animal — not head-on, not with directness, but with patience and stillness and the willingness to wait. Maybe he just doesn't have the words left. He's spent them all tonight, and the silence that follows is the same warm, impossible silence from before, only now our hands are holding the weight of it.
We stand there. The wind blows. The city pulses. The hum settles in my throat, and the copper taste fades to something I can almost ignore.

I have told him my deepest fear without explaining what it means. He has told me he's been searching for my soul without knowing it. We are standing three inches apart at the edge of a building, and the whole truth — the four-hundred-year, shape-shifting, body-altering, abomination-making truth — is right there, pressing against the back of my teeth like a scream.
And the hum will not let me say it.

So I hold his hand instead. And I watch the city. And I let the silence hold us both, warm and dangerous, the most comfortable silence I've had in four hundred years, and the most afraid I've ever been.
